facts.text: Lake Placid became famous worldwide after hosting the 1932 and 1980 Winter Olympics, making it one of only four cities to do so twice.
The Lake Placid Club, founded by Melvil Dewey in 1895, was an exclusive resort that helped launch the town’s reputation as a winter sports hub.
Lake Placid’s historic John Brown Farm State Historic Site preserves the home and gravesite of the 19th‑century abolitionist who chose to be buried on his own property.
The village’s first industry was iron‑ore mining, but by the 1840s it had shifted to a summer resort, attracting wealthy visitors to its Adirondack Great Camps.
